title: STL Containers - vector, list, map, set, stack, queue

seo.title: C++ STL Containers - vector, list, map, set, stack, queue, priority_queue | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master C++ STL containers: vector, list, deque, set, multiset, map, multimap, unordered_set, unordered_map, stack, queue, and priority_queue. Includes time complexity comparison, custom comparators, and 62+ practice questions for competitive programming.
seo.keywords: c++ stl containers, c++ vector, c++ map, c++ set, c++ stack, c++ queue, c++ priority_queue, c++ unordered_map, c++ multiset, competitive programming c++ containers

content.what:
What Are STL Containers?

The Standard Template Library (STL) provides a collection of generic, type-safe container classes that store and organize data. Instead of writing your own linked list, binary search tree, or hash table from scratch, you use battle-tested containers like vector, set, map, and unordered_map that are already optimized and correct.

STL containers are divided into four categories:

  • Sequence containers -- store elements in a linear order: vector, list, deque
  • Associative containers -- store elements in sorted order using a balanced BST: set, multiset, map, multimap
  • Unordered containers -- store elements using hash tables for O(1) average access: unordered_set, unordered_map
  • Container adaptors -- wrap other containers with a restricted interface: stack, queue, priority_queue
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <set>
#include <map>
#include <stack>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // Sequence: ordered by insertion
    vector<int> v = {5, 2, 8, 2, 1};

    // Associative: sorted, unique
    set<int> s(v.begin(), v.end());
    // s = {1, 2, 5, 8} -- sorted, duplicates removed

    // Associative: key-value, sorted by key
    map<string, int> marks;
    marks["Arjun"] = 85;
    marks["Priya"] = 92;

    // Container adaptor: LIFO
    stack<int> st;
    st.push(10);
    st.push(20);
    cout << st.top() << endl;  // 20
    return 0;
}

When Rohan says "use a map for frequency counting" or Meera says "use a priority_queue for the greedy approach," every competitive programmer immediately knows the data structure and its time complexities.
content.why:
Why Are STL Containers Important?

1. Competitive Programming Efficiency

In competitive programming, choosing the right container is half the solution. Need O(log n) lookups with sorted order? Use set or map. Need O(1) average lookups? Use unordered_map. Need a max-heap? Use priority_queue. Knowing which container to use and its time complexity directly determines whether your solution passes within the time limit.

2. Correct by Construction

STL containers handle memory management, resizing, rebalancing, and rehashing automatically. A vector grows dynamically, a set stays balanced, and an unordered_map rehashes when the load factor is too high. Implementing these from scratch is error-prone and time-consuming.

3. Type Safety and Generic Programming

STL containers are templates -- vector<int>, map<string, int>, set<pair<int,int>> -- providing compile-time type checking. You cannot accidentally insert a string into a vector<int>.

4. Interoperability with STL Algorithms

All STL containers expose iterators, making them compatible with STL algorithms like sort, find, accumulate, and transform. This uniform interface is one of the most powerful features of the STL.

5. Interview and Placement Relevance

Companies like Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Flipkart, and Razorpay expect fluency with STL containers. Interview problems frequently require choosing the right container, understanding time complexities, and using containers like map, set, and priority_queue effectively.

codeExamples.title: Vector: Dynamic Array with Random Access
codeExamples.explanation: Vector is the most commonly used container in C++. push_back is amortized O(1), random access via [] or at() is O(1). The key difference: [] has no bounds checking while at() throws out_of_range. Use reserve() when you know the approximate size upfront to avoid repeated reallocations.
codeExamples.output:
v[1] = 20
v.at(2) = 30
Size: 3
Capacity: 4
After reserve(100), Capacity: 100
10 20 30 0 0
After pop_back, size: 4
After clear, size: 0

codeExamples.title: Set and Multiset: Sorted Unique and Sorted with Duplicates
codeExamples.explanation: A set stores unique elements in sorted order using a balanced BST (usually red-black tree). All operations -- insert, find, count, erase, lower_bound, upper_bound -- are O(log n). A multiset allows duplicates while maintaining sorted order. Use set when you need sorted unique values and O(log n) lookups.
codeExamples.output:
Set: 1 2 5 8
After insert 3 and 2: 1 2 3 5 8
3 found
count(2) = 1
lower_bound(3) = 3
upper_bound(5) = 8
Multiset: 1 2 2 5 5 8
count(2) = 2

codeExamples.title: Map and Multimap: Key-Value Pairs Sorted by Key
codeExamples.explanation: A map stores key-value pairs sorted by key using a balanced BST. All operations are O(log n). The [] operator is dangerous because it inserts a default-constructed value if the key does not exist. Use find() or at() for safe access. The frequency counting pattern (freq[c]++) is one of the most common uses of map in competitive programming.
codeExamples.output:
Arjun: 85
Kiran: 78
Neha: 88
Priya: 92
Ravi (auto-created): 0
Found Priya: 92
 :1 d:1 e:1 h:1 l:3 o:2 r:1 w:1
Arjun count: 2

codeExamples.title: Unordered Map and Unordered Set: O(1) Average Lookups
codeExamples.explanation: Unordered containers use hash tables for O(1) average-case insert, find, and erase. They are faster than set/map for most cases but do not maintain sorted order. The Two Sum problem is a classic example where unordered_map gives an O(n) solution instead of O(n log n) with sorted containers.
codeExamples.output:
Contains 3? Yes
Frequency of 'e': 2
Pair found: [0, 1]

codeExamples.title: Stack, Queue, and Priority Queue: Container Adaptors
codeExamples.explanation: Container adaptors provide restricted interfaces over other containers. stack is LIFO (push/pop/top), queue is FIFO (push/pop/front/back), and priority_queue is a max-heap by default. For a min-heap, pass greater<int> as the third template argument. All push/pop operations are O(log n) for priority_queue and O(1) for stack/queue.
codeExamples.output:
Stack top: 30
After pop, top: 20
Stack size: 2
Queue front: 10, back: 30
After pop, front: 20
Max-heap top: 50
After pop: 30
Min-heap top: 10

codeExamples.title: Priority Queue with Custom Comparator for Dijkstra-Style Processing
codeExamples.explanation: This shows the Dijkstra-style min-heap pattern using priority_queue with greater<pair<int,int>>. Pairs are compared lexicographically, so smallest distance comes first. The second example demonstrates a custom struct with a lambda comparator passed via decltype. This pattern appears in graph algorithms, task scheduling, and event-driven simulations.
codeExamples.output:
Processing order (shortest distance first):
Node 4, distance 1
Node 3, distance 2
Node 1, distance 5
Node 2, distance 8

Task processing order:
Deploy (priority 5)
Test (priority 4)
Debug (priority 3)

codeExamples.title: List and Deque: Insert/Remove at Both Ends
codeExamples.explanation: A list is a doubly-linked list with O(1) insert/erase at any position (given an iterator) but no random access. A deque (double-ended queue) supports O(1) push/pop at both ends AND random access via []. Use list when you need frequent insertions/deletions in the middle. Use deque when you need fast operations at both ends plus random access.
codeExamples.output:
List: 5 10 15 30 40
After remove(20): 5 10 15 30 40
Deque: 10 20 30 40 50
dq[2] = 30
After pop front and back: 20 30 40

codeExamples.title: Time Complexity Comparison of STL Containers
codeExamples.explanation: This reference program prints the time complexity of every major STL container operation. In competitive programming, choosing the wrong container (e.g., map when unordered_map suffices) can be the difference between TLE and AC. Memorize these complexities.
codeExamples.output:
=== STL Container Time Complexity ===

SEQUENCE CONTAINERS:
vector:  access O(1) | push_back O(1)* | insert O(n) | find O(n)
list:    access O(n) | push_front O(1)  | insert O(1) | find O(n)
deque:   access O(1) | push_front O(1)  | insert O(n) | find O(n)

ASSOCIATIVE CONTAINERS (balanced BST):
set:     insert O(log n) | find O(log n) | erase O(log n) | sorted
map:     insert O(log n) | find O(log n) | []/at O(log n) | sorted
multiset/multimap: same as set/map but allow duplicates

UNORDERED CONTAINERS (hash table):
unordered_set: insert O(1)* | find O(1)* | erase O(1)* | no order
unordered_map: insert O(1)* | find O(1)* | []/at O(1)* | no order
* = amortized / average case

CONTAINER ADAPTORS:
stack:          push O(1) | pop O(1) | top O(1)
queue:          push O(1) | pop O(1) | front O(1)
priority_queue: push O(log n) | pop O(log n) | top O(1)

commonMistakes.title: Using map[] for Lookup Instead of find()
commonMistakes.error: The [] operator on a map creates a new entry with a default-constructed value if the key does not exist. This silently inserts unwanted entries and can corrupt your data.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use find() or count() to check existence without inserting. The [] operator should only be used when you want to create or overwrite an entry.

commonMistakes.title: Modifying a set Element Directly
commonMistakes.error: Elements in a set are const because modifying them could break the sorted order maintained by the internal BST. The set would no longer be valid.
commonMistakes.explanation: To change a value in a set, you must erase the old value and insert the new one. This ensures the sorted invariant is maintained. The same applies to keys in a map.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting That priority_queue Is a Max-Heap by Default
commonMistakes.error: C++ priority_queue is a max-heap by default. The largest element is at the top. If you need a min-heap, you must explicitly specify greater<int>.
commonMistakes.explanation: The default priority_queue<int> uses less<int> internally, which creates a max-heap. For a min-heap, specify all three template parameters: priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>>.

commonMistakes.title: Iterating and Erasing from a Map/Set Simultaneously (Wrong Way)
commonMistakes.error: Erasing an element from a map/set invalidates the iterator pointing to that element. Incrementing an invalidated iterator is undefined behavior.
commonMistakes.explanation: In C++11 and later, erase() on associative containers returns an iterator to the next element. Use this return value instead of manually incrementing. Notice the ++it is in the else branch, not in the for loop header.

commonMistakes.title: Using unordered_map/unordered_set with Unhashable Types
commonMistakes.error: std::pair does not have a default hash function, so it cannot be used directly in unordered containers. You need a custom hash or use an ordered set instead.
commonMistakes.explanation: Unordered containers require a hash function for the key type. Built-in types (int, string) have default hashes, but pair, tuple, and custom structs do not. Either provide a custom hash functor or use the ordered variants (set, map) which only need operator<.

summary: Sequence containers store elements in insertion order. vector provides O(1) random access and amortized O(1) push_back but O(n) insertion in the middle. list provides O(1) insertion/deletion anywhere with an iterator but no random access. deque provides O(1) push/pop at both ends plus random access.

Associative containers (set, multiset, map, multimap) use balanced BSTs internally. All operations are O(log n). Elements are always sorted. set stores unique values, multiset allows duplicates, map stores unique key-value pairs, multimap allows duplicate keys.

Unordered containers (unordered_set, unordered_map) use hash tables. Average-case operations are O(1), worst-case O(n). They do not maintain any order. Use them when you need fast lookups and do not care about order.
Container adaptors wrap other containers with restricted interfaces. stack (LIFO), queue (FIFO), and priority_queue (heap). priority_queue is a max-heap by default; use greater<T> for a min-heap.
The map [] operator inserts a default value if the key does not exist. Use find() or count() for safe lookups. The at() method throws out_of_range if the key is missing.

For competitive programming: use vector for arrays, unordered_map for frequency counting, set for sorted unique elements, and priority_queue for greedy algorithms. Know the time complexity of every operation.
vector::reserve() pre-allocates memory without changing size. vector::resize() changes the size. Use reserve() when you know the approximate number of elements to avoid reallocations.
Elements in a set/map cannot be modified in place because it would break the sorted invariant. Erase the old element and insert the new one instead.
When erasing from a map/set while iterating, use the iterator returned by erase() to avoid iterator invalidation. The pattern is: it = container.erase(it).

Choose the right container based on your needs: sorted order with O(log n) -> set/map, O(1) average lookup -> unordered_set/unordered_map, LIFO -> stack, FIFO -> queue, priority processing -> priority_queue.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

The Standard Template Library (STL) provides a collection of generic, type-safe container classes that store and organize data. Instead of writing your own linked list, binary search tree, or hash table from scratch, you use battle-tested containers like vector, set, map, and unordered_map that are already optimized and correct.

STL containers are divided into four categories:

  • Sequence containers -- store elements in a linear order: vector, list, deque
  • Associative containers -- store elements in sorted order using a balanced BST: set, multiset, map, multimap
  • Unordered containers -- store elements using hash tables for O(1) average access: unordered_set, unordered_map
  • Container adaptors -- wrap other containers with a restricted interface: stack, queue, priority_queue
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <set>
#include <map>
#include <stack>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // Sequence: ordered by insertion
    vector<int> v = {5, 2, 8, 2, 1};

    // Associative: sorted, unique
    set<int> s(v.begin(), v.end());
    // s = {1, 2, 5, 8} -- sorted, duplicates removed

    // Associative: key-value, sorted by key
    map<string, int> marks;
    marks["Arjun"] = 85;
    marks["Priya"] = 92;

    // Container adaptor: LIFO
    stack<int> st;
    st.push(10);
    st.push(20);
    cout << st.top() << endl;  // 20
    return 0;
}

When Rohan says "use a map for frequency counting" or Meera says "use a priority_queue for the greedy approach," every competitive programmer immediately knows the data structure and its time complexities.

1. Competitive Programming Efficiency

In competitive programming, choosing the right container is half the solution. Need O(log n) lookups with sorted order? Use set or map. Need O(1) average lookups? Use unordered_map. Need a max-heap? Use priority_queue. Knowing which container to use and its time complexity directly determines whether your solution passes within the time limit.

2. Correct by Construction

STL containers handle memory management, resizing, rebalancing, and rehashing automatically. A vector grows dynamically, a set stays balanced, and an unordered_map rehashes when the load factor is too high. Implementing these from scratch is error-prone and time-consuming.

3. Type Safety and Generic Programming

STL containers are templates -- vector<int>, map<string, int>, set<pair<int,int>> -- providing compile-time type checking. You cannot accidentally insert a string into a vector<int>.

4. Interoperability with STL Algorithms

All STL containers expose iterators, making them compatible with STL algorithms like sort, find, accumulate, and transform. This uniform interface is one of the most powerful features of the STL.

5. Interview and Placement Relevance

Companies like Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Flipkart, and Razorpay expect fluency with STL containers. Interview problems frequently require choosing the right container, understanding time complexities, and using containers like map, set, and priority_queue effectively.

topicSpecific.hint: push_back adds to the end. Size increases by 1.
topicSpecific.explanation: push_back adds 40 to the end. The vector now has 4 elements: {10, 20, 30, 40}. v[3] = 40.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
set<int> s = {5, 2, 8, 2, 1, 5};
cout << s.size();
topicSpecific.hint: set stores unique elements only.
topicSpecific.explanation: set removes duplicates. The unique elements are {1, 2, 5, 8}, so size is 4.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
set<int> s = {30, 10, 20};
for (int x : s) cout << x << " ";
topicSpecific.hint: set maintains sorted order.
topicSpecific.explanation: set stores elements in sorted (ascending) order regardless of insertion order.

topicSpecific.hint: Stack is LIFO. top() shows the last pushed element.
topicSpecific.explanation: After pushing 10, 20, 30: top is 30. After pop, top becomes 20.

topicSpecific.hint: Queue is FIFO. front() is the first element pushed.
topicSpecific.explanation: Queue is FIFO. front() returns the first pushed (10), back() returns the last pushed (30).

topicSpecific.hint: map iterates in sorted order of keys.
topicSpecific.explanation: map stores entries sorted by key. "Arjun" comes before "Kiran" alphabetically.

topicSpecific.hint: reserve() sets capacity but does not change size.
topicSpecific.explanation: reserve(10) allocates memory for 10 elements but size remains 0 until elements are added. After two push_back calls, size is 2 but capacity is 10.

topicSpecific.hint: priority_queue is a max-heap by default.
topicSpecific.explanation: Max-heap: top is the largest element (50). After pop, the next largest (30) becomes top.

topicSpecific.hint: greater<int> creates a min-heap.
topicSpecific.explanation: With greater<int>, the priority_queue becomes a min-heap. The smallest element (10) is at the top.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
multiset<int> ms = {3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3, 5};
cout << ms.count(5) << " " << ms.count(7);
topicSpecific.hint: multiset allows duplicates. count() returns the number of occurrences.
topicSpecific.explanation: The value 5 appears 3 times in the multiset. The value 7 does not appear, so count is 0.

topicSpecific.hint: The [] operator on map creates a default entry if the key does not exist.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially 2 entries. Accessing m["Ravi"] with [] creates a new entry with default int value 0. Size becomes 3.

topicSpecific.hint: lower_bound returns first element >= value. upper_bound returns first element > value.
topicSpecific.explanation: lower_bound(4) points to 5 (first element >= 4). upper_bound(7) points to 9 (first element > 7).

topicSpecific.hint: map iterates in sorted order of keys.
topicSpecific.explanation: Map is sorted by key: {1:A, 2:B, 3:C}. begin() points to {1,A}. After two increments, it points to {3,C}.

topicSpecific.hint: resize(n, val) changes size. If growing, new elements get val. If shrinking, excess elements are removed.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially empty (size 0). resize(5, 42) fills with 42. resize(3) shrinks to first 3 elements (all 42).

topicSpecific.hint: push_front/push_back add to ends, pop_front/pop_back remove from ends.
topicSpecific.explanation: After push_front(10) and push_back(50): {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}. After pop_front and pop_back: {20, 30, 40}. front=20, back=40, dq[1]=30.

topicSpecific.hint: find returns an iterator. erase(iterator) removes that element.
topicSpecific.explanation: find(15) returns an iterator to 15. erase(it) removes it. Remaining sorted set: {5, 10, 20, 25}.

topicSpecific.hint: erase returns iterator to next element. Only erase even keys.
topicSpecific.explanation: Erase key 2 (even). Keys 1 and 3 remain. The it = m.erase(it) pattern safely iterates while erasing.

topicSpecific.hint: insert with initializer_list appends multiple elements.
topicSpecific.explanation: insert at end() appends {4, 5, 6}. Vector becomes {1,2,3,4,5,6}. Size=6, back()=6.

topicSpecific.hint: Min-heap with pairs. Pairs compare lexicographically (first element first).
topicSpecific.explanation: Min-heap: smallest pair is {1, "Arjun"} (compared by first element). top().second = "Arjun".

topicSpecific.hint: empty() returns true (1) if the vector has no elements.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially empty (true=1). After push_back, not empty (false=0). front() returns 10.

mixed.explanation: Two pop_back calls remove 5 and 4. Remaining: {1, 2, 3}. Size is 3, back() is 3.

mixed.hint: Inserting a duplicate into a set has no effect.
mixed.explanation: 20 already exists, so insert(20) is ignored. insert(40) adds a new element. Size: {10, 20, 30, 40} = 4.

mixed.hint: Stack is LIFO. pop() removes the top element.
mixed.explanation: After pushing A, B, C: top is C. pop() removes C. Top becomes B. Size is 2.

mixed.hint: freq[c]++ increments the count for each character.
mixed.explanation: In "banana": 'a' appears 3 times, 'n' appears 2 times, 'b' appears 1 time. Three unique characters, so map size is 3.

mixed.hint: erase removes the element. count checks if it exists.
mixed.explanation: erase(3) removes 3 from the set. count(3) returns 0 (not found). Size is now 4.

mixed.hint: insert(pos, val) inserts val before the given position.
mixed.explanation: insert at begin()+1 inserts 15 before index 1 (before 20). Result: {10, 15, 20, 30}.

mixed.hint: find() returns end() if the key is not found.
mixed.explanation: "Arjun" exists, so find returns a valid iterator (not end), comparison is true (1). "Kiran" does not exist, so find returns end, comparison is false (0).

mixed.hint: Max-heap pops the largest elements first. Stop when 3 elements remain.
mixed.explanation: Elements in max-heap order: 8, 7, 4, 3, 2, 1. Pop until 3 remain: pop 8 (sum=8), pop 7 (sum=15), pop 4 (sum=19). Remaining: {3, 2, 1}. Top = 3.
"Arjun" appears 3 times as a key. Total entries: 3 (Arjun) + 1 (Priya) = 4.

mixed.hint: lower_bound(25) returns first >= 25. upper_bound(30) returns first > 30. lower_bound(60) returns end() if no element >= 60.
mixed.explanation: lower_bound(25) -> 30 (first >= 25). upper_bound(30) -> 40 (first > 30). lower_bound(60) -> end() since 60 > all elements.

mixed.hint: [] overwrites existing values. erase removes by key. insert adds new entries.
mixed.explanation: m[2]=25 overwrites 20 with 25. erase(1) removes key 1. insert({4,40}) adds key 4. Sorted output: 2:25, 3:30, 4:40.

mixed.question: When would you choose unordered_map over map?
mixed.hint: Think about time complexity and ordering requirements.
mixed.answer: unordered_map provides O(1) average-case for insert, find, and erase, compared to O(log n) for map. Choose unordered_map when you do not need sorted order and want faster operations. Choose map when you need sorted iteration, lower_bound/upper_bound, or when the key type does not have a hash function.
mixed.explanation: In competitive programming, unordered_map is preferred for frequency counting and hash-based lookups. However, map is needed when the problem requires sorted order or range queries. Also, unordered_map has O(n) worst-case due to hash collisions, which can be exploited by adversarial inputs in some judges.

mixed.question: Why does the erase-iterate pattern require special care with maps and sets? Show the correct pattern.
mixed.answer: When you erase an element from a map/set using an iterator, that iterator is invalidated. If you increment it after erasing, the behavior is undefined. The correct pattern is: it = m.erase(it) which returns an iterator to the next valid element. Alternatively, use m.erase(it++) which copies the iterator, increments the original, then erases the copy.
mixed.explanation: This is a common bug in competitive programming and interviews. The key insight is that erase() on associative containers returns the next iterator in C++11 and later. Use it = m.erase(it) in the branch that erases, and ++it in the else branch.

mixed.hint: count returns 1 if key exists, 0 if not.
mixed.explanation: Three entries. "b" exists (count=1). "d" does not exist (count=0).

mixed.hint: set_intersection finds common elements between two sorted ranges.
mixed.explanation: The intersection of {1,2,3,4,5} and {3,4,5,6,7} is {3,4,5}. set_intersection works because sets are sorted.

mixed.hint: Stack is LIFO. Elements come out in reverse order.
Queue is FIFO. pop removes the front element.
mixed.explanation: FIFO: push Arjun, Priya, Kiran. pop removes Arjun (front). New front is Priya. Size is 2.

mcqs.question: Which STL container stores elements in sorted order with no duplicates?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. set stores unique elements in sorted order using a balanced BST. unordered_set is unsorted, vector and list maintain insertion order.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of push_back on a vector (amortized)?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. push_back is amortized O(1). Occasionally it triggers a reallocation (O(n)), but averaged over many operations, it is O(1).

mcqs.question: Which container follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. stack is LIFO -- the last element pushed is the first to be popped. queue is FIFO.

mcqs.question: What does v.at(i) do differently from v[i] for a vector?

options.B: at(i) throws out_of_range if i is out of bounds
options.D: at(i) returns a reference while [] returns a copy

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. at(i) performs bounds checking and throws std::out_of_range if the index is invalid. [] has no bounds checking and causes undefined behavior on invalid access.

mcqs.question: What is the default behavior of priority_queue in C++?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. priority_queue is a max-heap by default. The largest element is always at the top. Use greater<T> to create a min-heap.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of find() on a set?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. set uses a balanced BST internally, so find() is O(log n). For O(1) average lookup, use unordered_set.

mcqs.question: What happens when you use map[key] and the key does not exist?

options.C: Inserts a new entry with a default-constructed value

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The [] operator on a map inserts a default-constructed value (0 for int, empty string for string) if the key does not exist. This is a common source of bugs.

mcqs.question: Which container provides O(1) push/pop at both ends AND random access?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. deque supports O(1) push_front/push_back, pop_front/pop_back, AND random access via []. vector does not have efficient push_front. list does not have random access.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between set and multiset?

options.A: set is sorted, multiset is unsorted
options.B: multiset allows duplicate elements, set does not
options.C: multiset uses a hash table, set uses a BST
options.D: set allows duplicates, multiset does not

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Both set and multiset are sorted and use balanced BSTs. The only difference is that multiset allows duplicate elements.

mcqs.question: How do you create a min-heap using priority_queue?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The full template is priority_queue<type, container, comparator>. Using greater<int> reverses the comparison, making the smallest element the top.

mcqs.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of find() on unordered_map?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. unordered_map has O(1) average-case but O(n) worst-case due to hash collisions. If all keys hash to the same bucket, every operation degrades to linear time.

mcqs.question: Why can you NOT use pair<int,int> as a key in unordered_map without a custom hash?

options.B: pair does not have a default std::hash specialization
options.C: unordered_map does not support composite keys
options.D: pair is not a standard type

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. unordered_map requires std::hash<Key> to be defined. There is no standard hash for std::pair. You must provide a custom hash functor or use map which only requires operator<.

mcqs.question: What does vector::reserve(100) do?

options.A: Sets the size to 100 and fills with zeros
options.B: Allocates memory for at least 100 elements without changing size
options.C: Limits the vector to a maximum of 100 elements

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. reserve() only allocates memory (sets capacity) without changing the size or adding elements. Use resize() to actually change the size.

mcqs.question: What internal data structure does std::set use?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. All major implementations (GCC libstdc++, Clang libc++, MSVC) use a red-black tree for set, map, multiset, and multimap. This guarantees O(log n) operations.

mcqs.question: Which container adaptor should you use for BFS (Breadth-First Search)?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. BFS requires FIFO processing: explore nodes in the order they are discovered. queue provides exactly this behavior. stack would give DFS. priority_queue is used for Dijkstra's algorithm.

mcqs.question: What does v.clear() do on a vector?

options.B: Removes all elements, size becomes 0, capacity may remain

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. clear() removes all elements and sets size to 0, but the allocated memory (capacity) is typically not freed. To free memory, use the swap trick: vector<int>().swap(v).

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of push and pop on a priority_queue?

options.B: O(log n) for push, O(1) for pop
options.C: O(log n) for both push and pop
options.D: O(n) for push, O(log n) for pop

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. priority_queue uses a binary heap. Both push (sift-up) and pop (sift-down) require O(log n) time. top() is O(1).

mcqs.question: Which container should you use for frequency counting when you need sorted output?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. map stores key-value pairs sorted by key. When you iterate over a frequency map, keys appear in sorted order. unordered_map gives faster counting but unsorted iteration.

codingChallenges.title: Frequency Count and Print in Sorted Order
codingChallenges.description: Given a string, count the frequency of each character and print the characters in sorted order along with their counts.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use a map<char, int> for automatic sorted order.

codingChallenges.title: Check If Parentheses Are Balanced Using Stack
codingChallenges.description: Given a string containing parentheses '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', determine if the input string is valid. An input string is valid if open brackets are closed by the same type and in the correct order.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use a stack. Time complexity O(n).

codingChallenges.title: Two Sum Using Unordered Map
codingChallenges.description: Given a vector of integers and a target, find two indices such that the numbers at those indices add up to the target. Assume exactly one solution exists.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use unordered_map for O(n) time complexity.

codingChallenges.title: Merge K Sorted Arrays Using Priority Queue
codingChallenges.description: Given K sorted arrays, merge them into one sorted array. Use a min-heap (priority_queue with greater<>) to always extract the smallest current element.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use priority_queue<tuple<int,int,int>, vector<...>, greater<...>> storing {value, array_index, element_index}. Time: O(N log K).

codingChallenges.title: Find First Non-Repeating Character Using Map and Queue
codingChallenges.description: Given a stream of characters, find the first non-repeating character at each step. If all characters repeat, print -1.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: a -1 b b b b d
codingChallenges.constraints: Use a queue to track order and an unordered_map for frequency. Process character by character.

codingChallenges.title: Find K Closest Points to Origin Using Max-Heap
codingChallenges.description: Given n points in 2D space, find the K closest points to the origin (0, 0). Use a max-heap of size K to maintain the K closest points seen so far.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use priority_queue with custom comparator. Time: O(n log K).
